title: Client Report - Joins
text:
QUESTION 1
Write an SQL query that pulls in the the salaries table and the collegeplaying table (and any other tables you might need) and store them in polars dataframes. In Python Polars, combine the dataframes to create a list of baseball players who attended BYU-Idaho. The new table should contain five columns: playerID, schoolID, salary, yearID and teamID associated with each salary.
For this question, I first read in the two tables and then joined them and filtered through them, giving me a list of all baseball players who attended BYU Idaho. I then filtered out the columns I wanted to view, giving me the final results for all players who attened BYUI in an easy to read table.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
q = """
    SELECT * FROM salaries
"""
salaries = pl.read_database(q, con)

q = """
    SELECT * FROM collegeplaying
"""
collegeplaying = pl.read_database(q, con)

# Keep the most recent salary per player (by yearID) to avoid multiple rows per player
salaries_latest = salaries.sort("yearID", descending=True).unique(subset=["playerID"])

# Keep the most recent college record per player as well
collegeplaying_recent = collegeplaying.sort("yearID", descending=True).unique(subset=["playerID"])

# Join on playerID and filter for BYU-Idaho; ensure final rows are unique per playerID
together = salaries_latest.join(collegeplaying_recent, on="playerID", how="inner")
result = (
    together
    .filter(pl.col("schoolID") == "idbyuid")
    .select(["playerID", "schoolID", "salary", "yearID", "teamID"])
    .unique(subset=["playerID"])
)
print(result)

title: Client Report - Joins
text:
QUESTION 2
Pick any two baseball teams and compare them using a metric of your choice (average salary, home runs, number of wins, etc). Be creative! Write an SQL query to read in the table(s) you need, then use polars to manipulate the data. Finally, make a graph using Lets-Plot to visualize the comparison. What do you learn?
I decided to see what the average attendance of a baseball game was for two teams, the New York Yankees and the Houton Astros. I thought that the teamsfranchises table would be helpful to call the teams their franchise names in the graph but I ended up not using it and just manipulaiting the graph details themselves. We can learn here that the astros typically have more fans attending their games.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
q = """
 SELECT teamkey, games, attendance, yearkey 
 FROM homegames
 WHERE yearkey > 1965
"""
attendance = pl.read_database(q, con)
q = """
SELECT franchNAME, franchID 
FROM teamsfranchises
"""
franch = pl.read_database(q, con)
result = attendance.join(franch, left_on="teamkey", right_on="franchID", how="left")
result = result.with_columns(one_game=(pl.col('attendance') / pl.col('games')).round(0)).filter(pl.col("teamkey").is_in(["HOU", "NYA"]))

(ggplot(result, aes(x="yearkey", y="one_game", color="teamkey"))
    + geom_line(size=1)
    + scale_color_manual(values={"NYA": "navy", "HOU": "orange"})
    + labs(
        title="Average Game Attendance Since 1965",
        x="Year",
        y="Avg Attendance per Game",
        color="Team"
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(format="{d}")
)

QUESTION 1
Use the provided code above to read in the batting table. Then, with Python Polars manipulate the вҖҳresultsвҖҷ dataframe to calculate the number of at bats and the batting average for each year a player played. Filter the data frame to only include players with at least 10 at bats that year. Sort the dataframe from highest batting average to lowest, and then by playerid alphabetically. Show the top 5 results in your report.
Note: A batting average is meant to measure how often a player puts the ball in play and gets to base safely. For this task, use a simplified version of batting average defined as the sum of hits (labeled вҖҳHвҖҷ) divided by number of at bats (labeled вҖҳABвҖҷ).
Here we can create a new column, using the already established columns for hits and at bats to calculate and save the batting average. Then we filter out the rows in which players had less than 10 at bats and sort the columns by batting average and then their player id. Here we find the highest batting average happened in 1929 with a .51 batting average.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
results_per_year = results.with_columns(batting_average = pl.col("H") / pl.col("AB")).filter(pl.col('AB') > 10).sort(["batting_average", 'playerID'], descending = [True, False])

print(results_per_year.select("playerID", 'yearID', "AB", 'H', "batting_average").head(5))

QUESTION 2
Use the provided code above to read in the batting table. Then, with Python Polars manipulate the вҖҳresultsвҖҷ dataframe to calculate the batting average for each player over their entire career (all years combined). Only include players with at least 100 at bats over their entire career. Sort the dataframe from highest batting average to lowest, and then by playerid alphabetically. Show the top 5 results in your report.
Note: A batting average is meant to measure how often a player puts the ball in play and gets to base safely. For this task, use a simplified version of batting average defined as the sum of hits (labeled вҖҳHвҖҷ) divided by number of at bats (labeled вҖҳABвҖҷ).
For this table I used practically the same methodology. The only thing that changed is I made sure to sum the at bats and hits for each player over all the years and then the table could be filtered by at bats (more than 100) and sorted.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
results_total = results.group_by('playerID').agg(pl.col('H').sum().alias('total_hits'), (pl.col('AB').sum().alias("at_bat"))).with_columns(batting_average = pl.col("total_hits") / pl.col("at_bat")).filter(pl.col('at_bat') >= 100).sort(["batting_average", 'playerID'], descending = [True, False])

print(results_total.select("playerID", "at_bat", 'total_hits', "batting_average").head(5))

title: Client Report -How good is it, really?
text:
QUESTION 1

Describe the quality of your classification model using 2-3 different evaluation metrics. You also need to explain how to define/interpret each of the evaluation metrics you use.

Here I use 3 different tests to see how accurate and precise my model is. First we use the accuracy score, then I pull in a classification report which is able to read through both the precision, f1-score, and recall of the model. I also added in a confusion matrix because I thought it was intriguing to see how many of the predictions. The top left block shows us how many were true negatives, the top right shows false negatives, the bottom left shows false positives and the bottom right shows true positives. Here weвҖҷre able to see how many cases are incorrect.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
# Don't forget, you need another import statement to import the desired ML model from sklearn
feature_names = [
  'livearea','finbsmnt','basement','stories','nocars','numbdrm','numbaths','condition_AVG',
  'quality_A','quality_B','quality_C','quality_D','quality_X',
  'gartype_Att','gartype_Det','gartype_None','gartype_CP'
]
features = dwellings.select(feature_names).to_numpy()
target = dwellings.select('before1980').to_numpy().ravel()
x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(features, target, test_size=0.3, stratify=target)
model = RandomForestClassifier()
model.fit(x_train, y_train)
y_pred = model.predict(x_test)


acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print('Accuracy:', acc)

print('Classification report:')
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred))

print('Confusion matrix:')
cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred)
print(cm)

QUESTION 2

Justify your classification model by discussing the most important features selected by your model. This discussion should include a plot of permutation importance values for the most important features. Were there any features you thought would be importance but werenвҖҷt?

I ran the permutation test and ploted the top 10 features. In the last task I guessed that the living area, finished basement, and condition of the house would be the most important features. While they were all in the top ten, I thought that the condition of the house would be more important than it was. I was surprised that the number of stories came out as number one.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
r = permutation_importance(model, x_test, y_test, n_repeats=10, random_state=0, n_jobs=1)

importances = pd.Series(r.importances_mean, index=feature_names)
top = importances.nlargest(10)

df_perm = pd.DataFrame({
  'feature': top.index,
  'importance': top.values,
})
df_perm['feature'] = pd.Categorical(df_perm['feature'], categories=df_perm['feature'], ordered=True)

(ggplot(df_perm, aes(x='feature', y='importance', fill='feature'))
 + geom_bar(stat='identity', fill = "blue")
 + labs(
     title='Top 10 Permutation Importances',
     x='Feature',
     y='Mean accuracy decrease'
   )
)

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Executive Summary

This report uses the FiveThirtyEight Star Wars survey to predict whether a respondentвҖҷs household income is above $50,000 using a Random Forest Classifier.
The dataset includes 835 respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film. Survey responses were cleaned and encoded for use in the model.
The model achieved approximately 65% accuracy on the test set.
The stretch was completed: two charts from the article are recreated and validate the dataset.

Rows where no income was reported are removed. For other missing values, we filled it with a 0 before passing to the model. Any remaining string inputs that couldnвҖҷt be mapped to an integer are one-hot encoded, converting them to binary columns. Here weвҖҷre able to create our Machine Learning model to predict if a respondentвҖҷs household income is over $50,000. We remove the rows where the respondents didnвҖҷt record their income.Then we choose our features and target. We then split the data 70/30 and created our model. The modelвҖҷs accuracy is shown below.


Show the code
# One-hot encode remaining strings
str_cols = df_for_ml.select(cs.string()).columns
df_for_ml = df_for_ml.to_dummies(columns=str_cols)

# Remove rows where income was not reported
df_model = df_for_ml.filter(pl.col("high_income").is_not_null())

# Set features
feature_cols = [c for c in df_model.columns if c not in ["high_income", "income_num"]]

features = df_model.select(feature_cols).fill_null(0).to_numpy()
target = df_model.select("high_income").to_numpy().ravel()

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(features, target, test_size=0.3)

# Create model
model = RandomForestClassifier()
model.fit(x_train, y_train)

y_pred = model.predict(x_test)

acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print('accuracy:', acc)

title: Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies
text:
Conclusion
The model reached around 65% accuracy predicting whether a Star Wars fan earns over $50,000. The biggest limitation is that this is self-reported data, so people may not answer income questions accurately or at all. Going forward, it would be interesting to see if adding more demographic features or trying a different model type could push accuracy higher. The Star Wars data is fun to work with but probably isnвҖҷt the most reliable dataset for income prediction, but it is a good proof of concept that survey data can be used for this kind of prediction.

title: Client Report - Flights: Column Aggregation
text:
QUESTION 2
Which airport has the worst delays? Describe the metric you chose, and why you chose it to determine the вҖңworstвҖқ airport. Your answer should include a summary table that lists (for each airport) the total number of flights, total number of delayed flights, proportion of delayed flights, and average delay time in hours.
I decided to sort my table by the percentage of flights which were delayed and then by the amount (in hours) of an average delay. Here we can see which airports delay the highest amount of flights - and the average delay also seems to correlate with the highest percentage of flights.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
worst = (
  df
  .with_columns(pl.selectors.starts_with("num_of").cast(pl.Int64, strict=False)).filter(~pl.any_horizontal(pl.selectors.starts_with("num_of") == -999))
  .group_by("airport_code")
  .agg(pl.col("num_of_delays_total").sum(), pl.col("num_of_flights_total").sum(), pl.col("minutes_delayed_total").sum())
  .with_columns(pct_delayed = pl.col("num_of_delays_total") / pl.col("num_of_flights_total"), avg_delay = (pl.col("minutes_delayed_total")/ 60 / pl.col("num_of_delays_total"))))

print(worst.sort("pct_delayed", "avg_delay", descending= True))

title: Client Report - WhatвҖҷs in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 1

How does your name at your birth year compare to its use historically? Your must provide a chart. The years labels on your charts should not include a comma.
Here this red dot marks the year that I was born. We can see that my name was used when it was almost at its peak and was very popular in the years surrounding my birth year.


Show the code
# Q1
ella = df.filter(pl.col("name")=="Ella")
(ggplot(ella, aes(x="year", y="Total")) 
+ geom_line() 
+ geom_point(data= ella.filter(pl.col("year") == 2005), color="red",size = 4)
+ labs(title = "Usage of the Name 'Tiana' Over Time", x="Year", y = "Usage of the name Ella")
+ scale_x_continuous(format="{d}")
)

title: Client Report - WhatвҖҷs in a Name?
text:
QUESTION 2
Think of a unique name from a famous movie. Plot the usage of that name and see how changes line up with the movie release. Does it look like the movie had an effect on usage?
The green dot marks when the movie вҖңPrincess and the FrogвҖқ was released, with the main character being named Tiana. We can see that after the movie was released, the name was used more frequently, but still not as frequently as the years before the movie was even released.

)

title: Client Report - WhatвҖҷs in a Name?
text:

Mary, Martha, Peter, and Paul are all Christian names. From 1920 - 2000, compare the name usage of each of the four names in a single chart. What trends do you notice?
While not all of the names have the same amount of uses, they have a similar trend in that between 1920 and 1970 they were used frequently and after 1970 they began to be used less and less frequently as the population drifted a little from common and traditional names.


Show the code
# Q3
old_names = df.filter(
  (pl.col("name").is_in(["Mary", "Martha", "Peter", "Paul"])))
(ggplot(old_names, aes(x="year", y="Total", color = "name")) 
+ geom_line()
+ labs(title = "Usage of the Names 'Mary', 'Martha', 'Peter', and 'Paul' Over Time", x="Year", y = "Total")

+ scale_x_continuous(limits=(1920,2000), format="{d}")
)

title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 1

Make a new column that represents the total number of babies with your name for only those states in which you have lived. (If you have only ever lived in one state, pick at least one other state so you get practice summing across columns). Make a graph displaying that total for your name over time.
Here we can see the usage of the name вҖңEllaвҖқ in the states Colorado, Texas, and Montana, all states in which I have already lived. The red dots signifies my birth year, in which we see a huge peak in the usage of the name вҖңEllaвҖқ


Show the code
# Q1
my_name = df.select(
  "name",
  cs.by_name("year", "CO", "TX", "MT")
).filter(pl.col("name")=="Ella").with_columns(total = pl.col("CO") + pl.col("TX") + pl.col("MT"))
dot = my_name.filter(pl.col("year") == 2005).with_columns(
    pl.lit("My Birth Year").alias("lab")
)

(ggplot(my_name, aes(x="year", y="total"))
+ geom_line()
+ geom_text(aes(label="lab"), nudge_y=50, color="red", data=dot)
+ geom_point(data=dot, color="red", size=4)
+ labs(title="Usage of the Name 'Ella' Over Time in States Colorado, Texas and Montana")
+ scale_x_continuous(format="{d}")
)

title: Client Report - Famous Names
text:
QUESTION 2

LetвҖҷs look at a name that is special for members of the church: Brigham. For each year, calculate what proportion of babies named Brigham were born in Idaho and Utah? Make a graph displaying the proportion over time.

This graph shows us the usage of the name вҖңBrighamвҖқ in the states of Idaho and Utah. We can see that since the late 2010вҖҷs, the usage of the name has been declining in these states.


Show the code
# Q2
brigham_name = df.select(
  'name',
  cs.by_name("UT", "ID", "year", "Total")
).filter(pl.col("name") == ("Brigham")).with_columns(total_new = pl.col("UT")+pl.col ("ID"))
brigham_name = brigham_name.with_columns(proportion = pl.col("total_new")/ pl.col("Total"))
(ggplot(
  brigham_name, aes(x = "year", y="proportion"))
+ geom_line()
+ labs(title = "Proportion of the name 'Brigham' in Utah and Idaho vs other states")
+ scale_x_continuous(format="{d}")
)

title: Client Report - Famous Names

Consider doing some data validation. For the name David, calculate a sum of all 50 states and District of Columbia for each year. Now compare that total to the value in the вҖҳTotalвҖҷ column. In which years, if any, do they not agree?

text:
QUESTION 3

According to the BTS website, the вҖңWeatherвҖқ category only accounts for severe weather delays. Mild weather delays are not counted in the вҖңWeatherвҖқ category, but are actually included in both the вҖңnasвҖқ and вҖңlate_aircraftвҖқ categories. Using the columns you modified in the bullet above, your job is to create a new column that calculates the total number of flights (in 1,000вҖҷs) delayed by weather (both severe and mild). Show your work by printing the first 5 rows of the dataset. Use these three rules for your calculations:

100% of delayed flights in the Weather category are due to weather

30% of all delayed flights in the вҖңlate_aircraftвҖқ category are due to weather

From April through August, 40% of delayed flights in the NAS category are due to weather. The rest of the months, the proportion rises to 65%


No discussion needed for this question


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
#df = df.with_columns(weather = (pl.col("num_of_delays_weather") + (pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft") * .3) + pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(["April", "May", "June", "July", "August"])).then(pl.col("num_of_delays_nas") * .4).otherwise(pl.col("num_of_delays_nas") * .65) ))
df = df.with_columns(
    total_weather_delays = (
        pl.col("num_of_delays_weather")
        + (pl.col("num_of_delays_late_aircraft") * 0.30)
        + pl.when(pl.col("month").is_in(["April", "May", "June", "July", "August"]))
            .then(pl.col("num_of_delays_nas") * 0.40)
            .otherwise(pl.col("num_of_delays_nas")* 0.65)
    )
)
print(df.select("month", "num_of_delays_weather", "num_of_delays_late_aircraft", "num_of_delays_nas", "total_weather_delays").head(5))

title: Client Report - Show me!
text:
QUESTION 1

Repeat the classification exercise using 2 different algorithms/models. Explain the differences between the 3 models (your original one, and the 2 new ones for this task).

1 Random Forest Classifier (original): Builds different decision trees and combines their answers. This model handles messy data well and doesnвҖҷt tend to overfit.
2 Logistic Regression: A simpler model that draws a straight line to separate the two groups. ItвҖҷs easier to understand but can struggle when data has complex patterns.
3 Gradient Boosting Classifier: Builds trees one at a time and each new tree fixes the mistakes of the previous one. ItвҖҷs usually very accurate but takes longer to train.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
# Model 1: Old Random Forest Classifier
rfmodel = RandomForestClassifier(random_state=42)
rfmodel.fit(x_train, y_train)
rf_pred = rfmodel.predict(x_test)
rf_acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, rf_pred)

# Model 2: Logistic Regression
lrmodel = LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=42)
lrmodel.fit(x_train, y_train)
lr_pred = lrmodel.predict(x_test)
lr_acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, lr_pred)

# Model 3: Gradient Boosting Classifier
gb_model = GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=42, n_estimators=100)
gb_model.fit(x_train, y_train)
gb_pred = gb_model.predict(x_test)
gb_acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, gb_pred)

print("Accuracy of the three different models:")
print(f"Random Forest:", rf_acc)
print(f"Logistic Regression", lr_acc)
print(f"Gradient Boosting:", gb_acc)

Use the same query as above, but only include players with at least 10 at bats that year. Print the top 5 results.
We can use the same logic as the before table with this query, only this time we filter out those who have been up to bat less than ten times, This decreases our averages shown in the top five because they had more chances at bat.

    SELECT playerID, yearID, H,  AB, CAST (H AS FLOAT) / AB AS batting
    FROM batting
    WHERE AB >= 10
    ORDER BY batting DESC, playerID
    LIMIT 5;
    """
results = pl.read_database(q, con)

title: Client Report - Baseball Database SQL Query
text:
QUESTION 3
Now modify the SQL query to calculate the batting average for players over their entire careers (all years combined). Only include players with at least 100 at bats over their entire career, and print the top 5 results.
Here we use the same logic, but group our players together by playerID and sum their hits and at bats. Now we can see their career stats, and the five highest career players.


Show the code
# Include and execute your code here
q = """
    SELECT playerID,  
    SUM(H) AS total_hits,
    SUM(AB) AS total_at_bat, 
    CAST (SUM(H) AS FLOAT) / SUM(AB) AS batting
    FROM batting
    GROUP BY playerID, yearID
    HAVING SUM(AB) >= 100
    ORDER BY batting DESC, playerID
    LIMIT 5;
    """
results = pl.read_database(q, con)
